title: Defining functions and return values

overview.why: Functions are the primary unit of reuse in Python. Every FastAPI route, Pydantic validator, and utility helper is a function. Understanding return None vs returning a value is critical — forgetting to return a value is a common source of None-related bugs.
where_used: FastAPI route handlers (every endpoint is a function)
utility helpers in any Python module

why_learning_this: Every FastAPI endpoint is a function — understanding def, parameters, and return is a baseline requirement
Returning multiple values (tuple unpacking) is used constantly in real backend code to return both a result and a status

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to return a value
common_mistakes.explanation: def process(x): x * 2 computes but discards the result; the function returns None. Fix: add return x * 2.

common_mistakes.title: Shadowing a built-in name
common_mistakes.explanation: def list(...) or def id(...) silently replaces the built-in. Use descriptive domain names like user_list or record_id.

common_mistakes.title: Calling the function before defining it
common_mistakes.explanation: Python executes top-to-bottom at module load time. A call before the def raises a NameError. Define functions before the code that uses them, or wrap them in a main() block.

recall_questions.q: What does a function return when it has no return statement?
What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
recall_questions.answer: A parameter is the placeholder name in the function definition; an argument is the actual value passed when the function is called.

recall_questions.q: How do you return multiple values from a Python function?
recall_questions.answer: Separate them with a comma in the return statement — Python packs them into a tuple, which the caller can unpack.

recall_questions.q: What happens to code that appears after a return statement in the same branch?
recall_questions.answer: It is never executed — return exits the function immediately.

practice_tasks.title: DSN parser
practice_tasks.prompt: Write parse_dsn(dsn) that takes a connection string like 'localhost:5432' and returns the host as a string and the port as an integer as two separate return values. Print both from the caller.
practice_tasks.solution:
def parse_dsn(dsn):
    host, port_str = dsn.rsplit(':', 1)
    return host, int(port_str)

host, port = parse_dsn('localhost:5432')
print(host)
print(port)
print(type(port))

code_walkthrough.focus: return sends the processed value back to the caller; without it, result would be None — the function would silently discard the work.

challenge.title: Response envelope builder
challenge.prompt: Write make_response(data, success=True) that returns two values: a dict {'data': data, 'ok': success} and an integer HTTP status code — 200 when success is True, 400 when False. Unpack both return values at the call site and print them.
challenge.solution:
def make_response(data, success=True):
    envelope = {'data': data, 'ok': success}
    status = 200 if success else 400
    return envelope, status

body, code = make_response({'id': 1, 'name': 'alice'})
print(body)
print(code)

err_body, err_code = make_response(None, success=False)
print(err_body)
print(err_code)
